content:
{
  "title": "Adaptive Error-Driven Feedback Prompting for Code Generation in LLMs",
  "problem": "Current code generation prompting methods for LLMs lack explicit feedback loops that adapt prompts based on code execution errors, leading to suboptimal performance on complex programming tasks. Existing approaches do not leverage fine-grained, automatic error feedback within the prompt to iteratively improve generation.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM code generation is critical for automation and developer productivity, but static or fixed prompting methods often fail to address syntax and semantic errors exposed only after execution. Integrating dynamic, error-driven feedback into the prompting process can directly target and correct failure modes. Recent advances in LLMs and reliable code execution sandboxes make it timely to close the loop between code, execution, and prompt adaptation.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting framework where the output code of the LLM is executed in a controlled environment, and any resulting error messages (from syntax, runtime, or failed test cases) are parsed and automatically formatted into targeted feedback. This feedback is then injected into a new prompt that explicitly instructs the LLM to revise its code based on the detailed error context. The process iterates, with each loop adapting the prompt to the most recent execution feedback, enabling targeted correction and improved code reliability.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate our method on HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS benchmarks, comparing against standard prompting, chain-of-thought, and self-revision-based methods. Metrics will include code correctness (pass@k), compilability, and edit-efficiency (number of iterations to correctness). Ablation studies will isolate contributions of feedback granularity and prompt adaptation strategies."
}
